speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number four, interview with Chaim Engel. You were living in Israel. And you were telling us your feelings about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I could never really adjust to the life in Israel, although Selma liked it. The, the kids liked it, but I couldn't find my place there. So as I say, I was first this [INAUDIBLE], but I, I wanted to go away then.

speaker: interviewee
text: So every, every week, you had one day off. So I took every second week, went two days. And I went looking around, what is this? So I went [INAUDIBLE]. I went to [NON-ENGLISH], what is not far from Netanya. There were some Dutch people there, what we know and things like that. And I tried to find a job, because I was in the hotel business. So I went as a waiter in a summer resort in Netanya. I took that. And it was for the summer. And I worked there. And did we live there? We moved to [NON-ENGLISH]. And I went from [NON-ENGLISH] about six kilometers from Netanya. And I worked there the whole summer. And I think she probably told you about. And we were-- not in that time yet-- to go to Israel, to America. But anyway, there was a woman. She had a vegetable and fruit store in [NON-ENGLISH], what is a village. And I say, in a village? How can you exist, fruit and vegetables in a store, in a place like that? Everybody grows his own. But it seems she makes a living there. So there was a Dutch family. And we call them mother and father. It was older than my age was now, I guess. And he said, oh, you should buy this store. That's a good store. If you don't buy it, I'll let my son buy that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't have really money, but we had the appliances. So I figured I'd sell the appliances. In Israel in that time, was appliances a lot of money, too. But in meantime, we had the appliances. The people what we rented the house from them, they used our appliances. Because it's [? better that works. ?] And so I said, I give you money that you can buy this business.

speaker: interviewee
text: I get some money. I sold something. I don't remember. We took a lot of things with us, because we took maybe a big case. How do you call it, [INAUDIBLE]? A container case.

speaker: interviewee
text: A container, a very big container with everything. We took everything with us. So we sold a lot of things. We made some money. And we're still some short some money. And he borrowed me some money. And I bought this business. And slowly, I build it up, that there was four villages around that I went, every other day I went to this villages once a week. And I build up a pretty good business. Because I made some money there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that make you happier being in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: It made me so much happier. I was my own boss. And I decide, I decided what I have to do. And nobody told me, do this, do that, and whatever they wanted me, things like that. So I was my own boss. It was not my definite goal, but was a bridge over to something what I really wanted, to go to America. I wanted to go to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I worked there for years and made a pretty good living. I could afford anything. A matter of fact, we could even send some money to America for us. We keep it. So when we came here, we had some money here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and as I say, till one day I came in some little bit of contact with somebody. He said, I can do something for you that you can go to America. So that was hard. I couldn't have an affidavit. It was very hard to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she probably told you there was an uncle of hers. He was struggling a lot. And he came to America. And he talked with some lawyer in Buffalo. And he was willing to give an affidavit for us. He probably told them our story. It seems like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, I don't do it normally. But in this case, he did it maybe for him more than for us. Because he didn't know us at all. Anyway, he gave us affidavit. And we could go to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Selma want to come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. She didn't want to go. She was, she was very happy. But in that time was a different time like now. In that time was it really like a pioneer, and that this-- how should I say that? It was, she was young. And she liked the adventure from the life, things like that. And I probably looked a little ahead. I said, well, that is not what I wanted to do anyway. And I didn't like-- I can say this, not a laundry boy. My work was mine. It was the, not the most important thing is, it's most important that by a year, I was already my own boss. Because I-- I-- I did some wholesale too, I bought. I build it up so much. I had a lot smaller that came buying from me. I brought sometimes whole trucks with bananas, with tomatoes. So I could sell it to people what couldn't afford to go there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we did pretty good. I couldn't complain on that. There's not-- financially, we did. It's not the reason we went away from Israel. And then when we got the affidavit, so we did to, to, to, to go in too short a time. Because the affidavit doesn't mean tomorrow you go on the, on the plane.

speaker: interviewee
text: And somehow when, and we came here to America. And as probably she told you, we planned to go direct to her brother in Canada, because we didn't have any address here. We didn't go to the man that gave us the affidavit. So, so it was her family what we knew from Israel what went a year before we went there. They went to America. And we were in contact with them. And we had written him the situation is we, we cannot, we cannot go to Canada. We have to come to the  States. And we don't have any address. They say, no problem. Come to us. And they were really poor. They lived in the railroad station somewhere-- a small house, two little rooms with two kids. And we went to them. And we were a week there. And then we rented a house Because, as I say, we had money. But we did [? like ?] don't have money, because that we kept that this to be just something to begin something here.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got-- he worked at [INAUDIBLE] as a clerk there. So he found me a job at [INAUDIBLE]. And I start to work. So there was help. We-- the expenses, the minimum expenses, they were covered. Did you like this country when you got here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know really too much about, because I know America. But I hear about America. But I don't know it. But that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak English?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I came to America, I had some lessons a little bit, but very little, not enough to make a big conversation. So my job, a matter fact, at [INAUDIBLE], was to take orders for groceries, because they delivered. So it was not to, to, to help people. I was taking orders. First of all, I didn't knows the items. My English was bad. But somehow I made it out. I worked for nine months there. I worked for nine months. Then there was a franchise, a franchise. You see, the big bread companies like Arnold, Pepperidge Farm, they don't have their drivers. They have-- they sell a route. And there was one what came to [INAUDIBLE]. And he came with Arnold's route. And he wants to sell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I figured, that sounds safe. Because it's every day comes back. I don't take big risk about, because I am not a risky man, you see? I did what I did in [NON-ENGLISH] only once in a lifetime. So I decided this. Now, I was afraid to lose this little money what we had. I was afraid to lose. So there's not a reason to jump into everything. But that sound to me safe. It was really hard work.

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, oh, I will come and get you something else. Let me [INAUDIBLE] Know the country more. Well, it was nine months here [INAUDIBLE]. So I bought this route. And I did very well with it. I had to get up two o'clock at night and work till noon-- of noon, of two o'clock. And it was a risky and hard work. You have [? get ?] every morning and go around. And I was young. And I figured, that is safe. And we did pretty good. We saved some money, too. And I build it up. I sold part of my route, what gave me some money back. And then later, I was looking for something not risky what I'm very familiar with and to go into something like that. I was looking in Stanford. I was looking for card shop and gift store. I figured, card shop and gift store sounds-- it's not so much risk if you know a little bit. I always have confidence in me. I'm [? no ?] sales, a sales person. I [? no ?] businessmen. I always had the confidence in me. There was no-- I have to know a material. I have to know what's going on. And so, I build it up. And I made, as I say, a good living. And then I start to look for, for some stores in Stanford, because that was my area where I went. And I had different offers. [? So ?] one day, I bought a store in Stanford, a card shop and gift store. Now, I didn't know at that time what a redevelopment is. And I didn't know that this area for redevelopment. Anyway, it was the prices I could afford to pay. And I brought this card shop. And I didn't know even a lot of things. For example, somebody asked me for thumbtacks. I never forget that. I didn't what he talking about. [INAUDIBLE] of Chinese. Anyway, we build it up, too. We remodeled it a little bit. And we made a good living. It was fairly hard work. But that was the first step out of that what I did. And then I figured, let's build it up. And I sell it again, buy a bigger business-- things like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So till [? it was ?] about 10, 11 years we were in this business. So we did a good living, and, and still looking-- we were looking already for something else. And then our ex-son-in-law, my daughter's husband-- first husband-- was manager of [INAUDIBLE] in Saybrook. And he was on the point to sell the business. And I found a buyer for my business. So we sold, I sold my business. And I bought the business in Saybrook, the jewelry store, and took my son-in-law, my ex-son-in-law, as partner. I figure, he has the knowledge. We have the money. And I'm a businessman. And I think that we'll make it out. And that worked for over 20 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: In meantime, they got divorced. But I could get along with him. And I figured, let's make a living here. So what happened between, I cannot help anything, though. And it worked right till I decided I want to retire. And so I figured, let him give-- to have the business for a certain price. We made it. And, and he pays me in so many years. He pays me whatever he doesn't have. And that went good for five years. It went good till, till he went bankrupt. So we lost a lot of money what he couldn't afford to pay us. But still, we made it. And he went bankrupt. And, and later, he started again to have this big store. It was a department store. He took a section there, and he still run one of the stores.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he didn't have, I guess, the business sense. Because it was a good business. We make two families a living, And we made even some money that can build up a nice business. It was a big business, [? big. ?] Are you glad you came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never regret it for a minute. I don't know. Let me put this way. You know, I think it's nice, the best thing I could do. But if you talk with my daughter, you will ask, she will probably say no. Afterwards, how she is now, I think she all right. But she had, the kids had difficulty with, with the language. They spoke only Hebrew-- not even Dutch anymore-- Hebrew. And they would put them in a class where they had-- she will tell you that. You know, we just talked about. They had a hard time. They had a  hard time, because didn't speak the English, the language. They went into the fifth grade or whatever the grade. And they missed out a lot. They didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: What about you and Selma? Was it hard for you to establish lives here? I mean, you found businesses, but to feel part of American life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which sense? I mean, I think we are--

speaker: interviewer
text: Of the, the Jewish community or the larger community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, how should I say? I feel happy with. I feel happy here. Surely, there are certain things what I would like different. But I'm happy I went away from Israel, very happy about. Because first of all, when I was in Israel, I had to go every [INAUDIBLE] six weeks to the military. And I had enough of it. I had enough of it. And, and I like the life here. I like the life here. We have a much better life, but that was not really the main reason. Financially we did in Israel good, too. It is like with the orthodox now, with the religious [? one. ?] We are free. We are not like that at all. I don't feel at home at all with that. So I'm happy I'm not an Israeli. In this [INAUDIBLE], in this [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So I am very happy. I think Selma is now happy, too. I guess so. I hope so. Do you think about how different your life would've been if it hadn't been for the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never thought about, but it surely be different. First of all, we'll have family to begin with, what we miss, surely. And I probably would have been more a professional, some kind education, more professional thing. I don't know. I'm happy as it is. I, I'm the type I don't look back too much in the back what, what will happen. You have to make the best you, you do.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think that your feelings about being Jewish have been affected by your experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: To be honest with you, culturally, I was pride I was a Jew. And also, immediately after the war, you had different ideas-- become this, become that. This idea [? fix ?] is not really a real idea. You have to be what you have, unless you're born different. You're born different. But to change it, I don't think that is. I'm culturally very much Jewish, although I'm not religious. And I go once in a while to the temple, not for religious purposes. I go just because we are part of, of, of the group, of, of Jews. But I never was-- although was thinking maybe in the beginning till after the war. But I'm, I'm really happy as it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were thinking after the war of not being Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: You were thinking a million ideas, you know, what you were different, things like that. I don't think that that's it. I, I couldn't be anything else. I couldn't be anything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: When people hear your story, what would you like them to learn?

speaker: interviewee
text: To learn? What I would like them to learn is to be tolerant and to be no hate mongers. I can never understand the people why they have the hate. You know, not that we are those, the ideal people. We have our faults, like everybody else, but that is the way you have to judge it-- is people. Everybody's different. Everybody has his own ideas. And you have to tolerate that. The tolerance is the most important thing, because you have so many religions. You have so many cultures. And if you don't tolerate, there's no existence. You cannot exist different.

speaker: interviewer
text: You tell your story in a lot of different places to a lot of different groups. Do you-- are you ever surprised by people's reactions to your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We mostly talk to children in school-- high school and things like that. Now, I think it makes [? them-- ?] we had the feeling always that hearing our story, some makes an impression. And it may be a little, a good seed for the future. They might think what can happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: A matter of fact, one time-- when we go to schools the teacher always gives an appointment or an assignment to the students to write us a letter. And we get, I don't know how many letters. Now, one letter we had from a mother from a child. And she tells her story. She says, when my son comes from school, I ask him how was school today? And always, the answer is, ah. That's all. The day when we spoke, he came home. He didn't even wait for a question from the mother. He says, Mother, you know what happened today? We had somebody what told us about a camp. And it was so Impressive and things like that. So we see how some makes a big impression. So even 5%, we are touch. We touch some, some percentage that they accept what we tell them. And it sticks in their mind. That maybe is a good thing for them, that they in the future might think differently, not hated. I don't know how it works out. But at least there is a seed what could bring something like that. So we have some satisfaction from that. The letters we are getting were all very nice. I kept them always. I kept them. I don't know how many I have, a whole shelf full. Is there anything you'd like to add to what you've told us so far?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope that people would learn from that what I just said-- tolerance and not hate mongers. Because all these deniers don't deny because they don't believe what happened. It's just hate mongers. And they wanted to have their hatred among the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a matter of fact, there was once-- these hate mongers have sent letters to different colleges to print their address. I have the letters. So when we got hold of it, we have written to the colleges. I said, how can you print something like that? The answer was that is the First Amendment, free speech. You can say what you want.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that is not true. That is not free speech. That is lies, what they tell.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's not a question that happen, not happen. I just-- I have here this week I got from the ADL. I got a letter with a speech from Mohamed whatever it is things. I don't know if you read it. If you read this letter, your hair gets stay up. Well, then we're all very glad you have told us your story. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. That is the grave of my mother in Poland, in Lodz. That was maybe a year after she died. She died in 1936. I am in the right side of the picture, and the left side is my younger brother, and my father in the back of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's name is Meier Engel. My name is Chaim. And my father's name is Samuel.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found it. I went to, to the concentration camp together with my brother. They picked me out to work there, and he went to the gas chamber. Later, I have to have sorted the clothes. And I found this picture with his clothes. My granddaughter went to visit in Poland. And because she saw this picture, we ask her to go to the cemetery and to find her grave. She found it. And as proof, she made pictures of it. That's the same picture what the other one is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which granddaughter was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken, our daughter's oldest daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did she--

speaker: interviewee
text: She went, she went in 19, 1990-- what year she? '92.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, two years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years ago. That's 1996. That is a picture. Somebody made a caricature for myself in 1945. I don't remember exactly who did it and how it comes, but that's the way I looked after the war. That's a, a document from the Polish consulate in Holland. When I arrived in Holland, I had to register with the Polish consulate in Holland. And this is, this is a picture for myself and the document what I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- I don't know-- from 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Chaim Engel. There's my signature there. That is also a Pol-- a document from the Polish consulate in Holland. Although Selma, my wife, is born in Holland and she was Dutch, because of me, she became Polish, Polish, too. And so it's the same kind of document as I have. That is a picture from myself with my wife, Selma, somewhere taken the family. I don't know which year that happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: In America?

speaker: interviewee
text: In America maybe a few years ago. I don't how old is this picture there? I don't know. That is a picture of our four grandchildren. The outside on the right side is [? Emmy ?] , is the eldest daughter from our son. And the next is--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Emmy-- Emily. The next one is [? Tagan, ?] our daughter's oldest daughter. The next one going to the left is Kendra, which is the youngest in our daughter. And the last one is Heidi, our youngest, our, our son's youngest daughter. That is our daughter with her new husband. Our daughter's name is Alida, and her husband's name is Gene.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gene? What's his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gene [? Berger. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken when she got married last July.
